keys = list(artists_listeners.keys())

score = 0

while True:
  first_artist = random.choice(keys)
  second_artist = random.choice(keys)

  while first_artist == second_artist:
    second_artist = random.choice(keys)

  while True:
    guess = input(f"\nWhich artist has more monthly Spotify listeners - 1. {first_artist.title()} or 2. {second_artist.title()}? ")
    guess_lower = guess.strip().lower()

    if guess_lower == 'quit':
      print("Thanks for playing!")
      quit()

    elif guess_lower not in ['1', '2', first_artist.lower(), second_artist.lower()]:
      print("Invalid input. Please enter the name of one of the two artists, type 'quit' to end the game or enter 1 or 2 to make a guess.")

    elif guess_lower == first_artist.lower() or guess_lower == '1' and artists_listeners[first_artist] > artists_listeners[second_artist]:
      print(f"You guessed correctly! {first_artist.title()} had {artists_listeners[first_artist] / 1e6:.1f}M monthly Spotify listeners and {second_artist.title()} has {artists_listeners[second_artist] / 1e6:.1f}M monthly Spotify listeners.")
      score += 1
      break

    elif guess_lower == second_artist.lower() or guess_lower == '2' and artists_listeners[second_artist] > artists_listeners[first_artist]:
      print(f"You guessed correctly! {second_artist.title()} had {artists_listeners[second_artist] / 1e6:.1f}M monthly Spotify listeners and {first_artist.title()} has {artists_listeners[first_artist] / 1e6:.1f}M monthly Spotify listeners.")
      score += 1
      break

    else:
      print(f"\nSorry, you guessed incorrectly. {first_artist.title()} had {artists_listeners[first_artist] / 1e6:.1f}M monthly Spotify listeners and {second_artist.title()} has {artists_listeners[second_artist] / 1e6:.1f}M monthly Spotify listeners.\n")
      score = 0
      play_again = input("Would you like to play again? (y/n/Ent) ")
      if play_again.lower() == 'n':
        print("Thanks for playing!")
        quit()

  print(f"\nYour score is {score}.")

how can i change the code so images appear of the two rappers that are being compared

To have images appear of the two rappers being compared, one way to approach it is to use a package like `Pillow` or `OpenCV` to display the images. 

Here's an example of how you could modify the code to display the images:

```python
from PIL import Image

# Define an images dictionary with the file paths of each rapper's image
# Change the file paths to the location of the images on your machine
images = {
    'Lil Baby': 'lil_baby.jpg',
    'Lil Durk': 'lil_durk.jpg',
    'Lil Keed': 'lil_keed.jpg',
    # etc.
}

# Modify the while loop to display the images of the two rappers being compared
while True:
    first_artist = random.choice(keys)
    second_artist = random.choice(keys)

    while first_artist == second_artist:
        second_artist = random.choice(keys)

    # Load the images of the artists
    first_image = Image.open(images[first_artist])
    second_image = Image.open(images[second_artist])

    # Display the images
    first_image.show()
    second_image.show()

    # Prompt the user to make a guess
    guess = input(f"\nWhich artist has more monthly Spotify listeners - 1. {first_artist.title()} or 2. {second_artist.title()}? ")

    # Close the images of the artists
    first_image.close()
    second_image.close()

    # The rest of the code remains the same
```

In this example, we defined an `images` dictionary that maps each artist to the corresponding image file path. Inside the while loop, we load the images of the two artists using `Pillow`'s `Image.open()` method and display them using the `show()` method. After the user makes a guess, we close the images using the `close()` method. 

Note that you'll need to have the images saved locally and adjust the file paths in the `images` dictionary to match the location of the images on your machine. Additionally, you may want to resize the images to a consistent size for display purposes.
is does PIL need to be installed first like for example pip

Yes, you will need to have `Pillow` installed in order to use the `Image` module. You can install it using `pip` by running:

```
pip install pillow
```

After installation, you can import the `Image` module as shown in the code example I provided.
so this should be added in the beginning?

Yes, you can add the following line at the beginning of your code to import the `Image` module from `Pillow`:

```python
from PIL import Image
```

Then, you can use the `Image` module to open and display the images in the way described in the previous answer. Don't forget to install `Pillow` first by running `pip install pillow` in your terminal or command prompt.
can you write the whole code, you dont need to write every artist

Sure, here's an example modified code with the images:

```python
import random
from PIL import Image

artists_listeners = {
    'Lil Baby': 30600740,
    'Lil Durk': 20244848,
    'Lil Keed': 2288807,
    # add more artists here
}

images = {
    'Lil Baby': 'lil_baby.jpg',
    'Lil Durk': 'lil_durk.jpg',
    'Lil Keed': 'lil_keed.jpg',
    # add more images here
}

keys = list(artists_listeners.keys())

score = 0

while True:
    first_artist = random.choice(keys)
    second_artist = random.choice(keys)

    while first_artist == second_artist:
        second_artist = random.choice(keys)

    # Load the images of the artists
    first_image = Image.open(images[first_artist])
    second_image = Image.open(images[second_artist])

    # Display the images
    first_image.show()
    second_image.show()

    while True:
        guess = input(f"\nWhich artist has more monthly Spotify listeners - 1. {first_artist} or 2. {second_artist}? ")
        guess_lower = guess.strip().lower()

        if guess_lower == 'quit':
            print("Thanks for playing!")
            quit()

        elif guess_lower not in ['1', '2', first_artist.lower(), second_artist.lower()]:
            print("Invalid input. Please enter the name of one of the two artists, type 'quit' to end the game, or enter 1 or 2 to make a guess.")

        elif guess_lower == first_artist.lower() or guess_lower == '1' and artists_listeners[first_artist] > artists_listeners[second_artist]:
            print(f"\nYou guessed correctly! {first_artist} had {artists_listeners[first_artist] / 1e6:.1f}M monthly Spotify listeners and {second_artist} had {artists_listeners[second_artist] / 1e6:.1f}M monthly Spotify listeners.")
            score += 1
            break

        elif guess_lower == second_artist.lower() or guess_lower == '2' and artists_listeners[second_artist] > artists_listeners[first_artist]:
            print(f"\nYou guessed correctly! {second_artist} had {artists_listeners[second_artist] / 1e6:.1f}M monthly Spotify listeners and {first_artist} had {artists_listeners[first_artist] / 1e6:.1f}M monthly Spotify listeners.")
            score += 1
            break

        else:
            print(f"\nSorry, you guessed incorrectly. {first_artist} had {artists_listeners[first_artist] / 1e6:.1f}M monthly Spotify listeners and {second_artist} had {artists_listeners[second_artist] / 1e6:.1f}M monthly Spotify listeners.\n")
            score = 0
            play_again = input("Would you like to play again? (y/n/Ent) ")
            if play_again.lower() == 'n':
                print("Thanks for playing!")
                quit()

    # Close the images of the artists
    first_image.close()
    second_image.close()

    print(f"\nYour score is {score}.")
```

In this example code, we added the import statement `from PIL import Image` to import the `Image` module from `Pillow`. We also added a new dictionary `images` that maps each artist to the corresponding file path of their image (make sure to replace the file paths with the actual file paths on your machine). Inside the while loop, we load and display the images before prompting the user to make a guess, and our code includes methods to close the images after they're displayed.
but i need to dowload the image first?

Yes, you will need to have the images saved locally on your computer in order to display them using the code I provided. You can download images of the artists you want to use and save them in the same directory as your Python script, or in any other location on your computer.

In the code, you should provide the appropriate file path to each image file in the `images` dictionary. For example, if you save the `lil_baby.jpg` image file in the same directory as your Python script, you can add the following code to the `images` dictionary:

```python
images = {
    'Lil Baby': 'lil_baby.jpg',
    # add more images here
}
```

Make sure that you put the correct file name and extension for each image file in the dictionary. Once you have the images saved and have specified their file paths, you should be able to run the code and see the images of the artists being compared.
‘lil_baby.jpg sothis needs to be replaced with the path

Yes, you need to replace the string `'lil_baby.jpg'` in the `images` dictionary with the actual file path to the image of Lil Baby on your computer. 

For example, let's say you have the image file saved in the same directory as your Python script. In this case, you can use the file name as the value in the `images` dictionary:

```
images = {
    'Lil Baby': 'lil_baby.jpg',
    # Add other artists and their image paths here
}
```

If you save the file in a different directory, you would need to provide the full file path. For example:

```
images = {
    'Lil Baby': '/path/to/images/lil_baby.jpg',
    # Add other artists and their image paths here
}
```

Make sure to replace `/path/to/images` with the actual file path to the directory where the image file is saved.

Yes, that looks correct assuming your image file is located at 'C:\Users\elias\Pictures\Album covers/lil_baby.jpg'. Make sure to use double backslashes instead of single backslashes in the file path so that Python can properly interpret it as a string. 

Additionally, you may want to make sure that the folder names and file extension are correct. In the file path you provided, the folder name is "Album covers" and the file extension is ".jpg". If any of these elements are not correct, the image file will not be found and an error will occur.

Yes, when you are specifying a file path in a string in Python, you need to use double backslashes instead of single backslashes to escape the backslash character. 

So in your file path, change the single backslashes to double backslashes, like this:

```python
'Lil Baby': 'C:\\Users\\elias\\Pictures\\Album covers\\lil_baby.jpg'
```

Alternatively, you can use forward slashes, which don't need to be escaped, like this: 

```python
'Lil Baby': 'C:/Users/elias/Pictures/Album covers/lil_baby.jpg'
``` 

Both of these file path formats should work in most common operating systems, including Windows and Unix-based systems.

    first_artist = random.choice(keys)
NameError: name 'keys' is not defined

The error message is telling you that the variable `keys` is not defined, which means it was likely not properly initialized or defined beforehand in your code.

The `keys` variable is used to store a list of the keys from the `artists_listeners` dictionary, so you need to make sure that this variable is defined before you attempt to use it in the `while` loop. 

One thing you can check is whether you have initialized the `keys` variable properly in your code. You can initialize it by calling the `keys` method on the `artists_listeners` dictionary, like this:

```python
keys = list(artists_listeners.keys())
```

Make sure that this line of code appears before the `while` loop where you are using the `keys` variable, so that it is properly initialized before being used.

If you have already initialized the `keys` variable and are still getting this error, make sure that there are no typos or syntax errors in your code that may be affecting the variable's definition. You may also want to check that the indentation in your code is correct and that the variable is in scope in the current context.
